**PAPER**

# Lysozyme-directed synthesis of platinum nanoclusters as a mimic oxidase†

Cheng-Ju Yu,<sup>‡a</sup> Tzu-Heng Chen,<sup>‡a</sup> Jhih-Yu Jiang<sup>a</sup> and Wei-Lung Tseng<sup>*ab</sup>

We present a simple, one-pot approach for synthesizing ultrafine platinum (Pt) nanoclusters (NCs) under alkaline conditions using lysozyme (Lys) as a template. From the analysis of the nanoclusters by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry, Lys-stabilized Pt NCs majorly consisted of Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters. The formation of Pt NCs was confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy. The maximal fluorescence of Pt NCs appears at 434 nm with a quantum yield of 0.08, a fluorescence lifetime of 3.0 ns, and excitation-dependent emission wavelength behavior. Pt NCs exhibit an intrinsic oxidase-like activity because Pt NCs can catalyze O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of organic substrates through a four-electron reduction process. Compared with larger Pt nanoparticles, the Pt NCs produce substantially greater catalytic activity in the O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, and dopamine.

## 1 Introduction

Molecule-like metal-based (Au, Ag, and Pt) nanoclusters (NCs), comprising several to tens of atoms, have attracted a considerable amount of attention in the fields of heavy metal and biomolecule sensing, catalysis, biological imaging and labeling, and fuel cells.<sup>1,2</sup> Compared to metal nanoparticles (NPs) with diameters greater than 2 nm, NCs exhibit greater catalytic activities and have discrete electronic structures.<sup>3</sup> A high percentage of the metal content of metal NCs is exposed to the nanoparticle surface. For example, in a 2.0 nm NC, 50% of the metal atoms are exposed. Consequently, metal NCs are highly active catalysts for several crucial reactions; Au NCs have been shown to catalyze the oxidation of carbon monoxide,<sup>4,5</sup> the reduction of 4-nitrophenol,<sup>6</sup> and the reduction of oxygen.<sup>7</sup> Pt NCs exhibit extraordinarily high levels of activity in oxidizing carbon monoxide,<sup>8</sup> reducing oxygen,<sup>9</sup> and oxidatively dehydrogenating propane.<sup>10</sup> Metal NPs, including Pt, Au, and Ag NPs, often exhibit peroxidase- and oxidase-like activities, and can catalyze the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of peroxidase substrates, the oxidation of glucose with co-substrate O<sub>2</sub>, and the oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of O<sub>2</sub>.<sup>11–15</sup> Au NCs exhibit peroxidase-like activity, and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>.<sup>16</sup> However, few studies have reported the use of metal NCs to mimic oxidase.

Although these NCs do not possess plasmonic properties, they interact with light and undergo electronic transitions between discrete energy levels. The emission wavelength of metal NCs is tunable from the near-infrared to ultraviolet (UV) regions by changing the number of metal atoms that are present within the clusters.<sup>17</sup> For example, poly(amidoamine) dendrimer-encapsulated Au<sub>5</sub>, Au<sub>8</sub>, Au<sub>13</sub>, Au<sub>23</sub>, and Au<sub>31</sub> clusters produce UV, blue, green, red, and near-IR emissions, respectively.<sup>18</sup> Moreover, Ag NCs with core sizes ranging from Ag<sub>2</sub> to Ag<sub>8</sub> emit over a range of 400 to 600 nm.<sup>19,20</sup> However, in all of the metal NCs, relatively few studies have been devoted to the preparation of fluorescent Pt NCs. Kawasaki *et al.* proposed a one-pot, surfactant-free approach for synthesizing fluorescent Pt NCs with 4 to 6 Pt atoms in a *N,N*-dimethylformamide solution without capping ligands.<sup>21</sup> Poly(amidoamine) dendrimers with terminating hydroxyl groups were used as templates to direct the growth of fluorescent Pt<sub>5</sub> clusters and non-fluorescent Pt NPs by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with NaBH<sub>4</sub>.<sup>22</sup> In another approach, a two-step process was developed to synthesize Pt NCs. First, nanometer-sized Pt particles were prepared by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with NaBH<sub>4</sub> in the presence of a capping agent, mercaptosuccinic acid.<sup>23</sup> The formed Pt particles were then etched to form Pt NCs by ligand exchange between the capping ligand and glutathione (GSH) on the Pt surface. However, these methods typically have long reaction times, produce bulky Pt NPs, require the use of toxic organic solvents, and require multiple steps.

Lysozyme (Lys) can act as both a reducing and stabilizing agent in producing a wide variety of NPs, including Au,<sup>24</sup> Ag,<sup>25</sup> Si,<sup>26</sup> and TiO<sub>2</sub>-based NPs.<sup>26</sup> Recently, Lys was successfully used as a template for synthesizing Au NCs. The resulting Lys-stabilized Au NCs were shown to inhibit bacterial growth,<sup>27</sup> detect Hg(II) and CH<sub>3</sub>Hg(I),<sup>28</sup> sense GSH,<sup>29</sup> and catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of TMB in the presence of graphene.<sup>30</sup> We report a one-pot synthesis of fluorescent Pt NCs through the reduction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys at a low temperature (37 °C). The as-prepared fluorescent Pt NCs produce an intense blue emission at 437 nm, and in the presence of dissolved O<sub>2</sub>, exhibit oxidase-like activity to oxidize ABTS, TMB, and dopamine.

## 2 Materials and methods

### Materials

Lys (chicken egg white) was purchased from MP Biomedicals (Irvine, CA). H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O, FeCl<sub>3</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O, FeCl<sub>2</sub>·4H<sub>2</sub>O, HAuCl<sub>4</sub>·3H<sub>2</sub>O, NaBH<sub>4</sub>, Na<sub>2</sub>HPO<sub>4</sub>, NaH<sub>2</sub>PO<sub>4</sub>, NaCl, dopamine, ABTS, TMB, methylene blue, sodium acetate, sodium citrate, catalase (9473 units per mg, from *Aspergillus niger*), horseradish peroxidase (HRP; 50–150 units per mg), and α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Milli-Q ultrapure water (Milli-pore, Hamburg, Germany) was used in all of the experiments.

### Characterization of Pt NCs

Absorption, fluorescence, and Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of the Pt NCs were recorded using a double-beam UV-visible spectrometer (Cintra 10e, GBC, Victoria, Australia), a Hitachi F-7000 fluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo electron Corporation, Madison, WI), respectively. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were conducted using an OB920 single-photon counting fluorometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments) with a pulsed nanosecond nitrogen lamp as the excitation source. The elemental compositions of the Pt NCs were obtained using JAMP-9500F auger electron spectroscopy (JEOL, Japan). Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) (Bruker Daltonics, Germany) was performed to determine the molecular weight of the Pt NCs. Prior to MALDI-TOF MS measurement, we mixed saturated α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid containing 70% acetonitrile with an equal volume of the purified Pt NCs and pipetted the resulting mixture onto a stainless steel 384-well target (Bruker Daltonics, Germany). In positive ion and reflection mode, each final mass spectrum was accumulated as an average of 2000 single shot spectra. Laser fluence was set to 72 μJ per 100 μm<sup>2</sup>. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1400; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the size and composition of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>, Pt, and Au NPs.

### Preparation of Pt NCs, Pt NPs, Au NPs, and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs

The preparation of the Pt NCs was performed by chemical reduction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys, as a soft template. A solution of Lys (5 mL, 1–25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>) reacted with an equal volume of 10 mM H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 2 min, followed by the rapid addition of NaOH (500 μL, 1 M). The obtained solution was incubated at 37 °C overnight. To remove excess Lys, a solution of Pt NCs was dialysed with 5 mM phosphate buffer (pH 3) for two days. The concentration of the obtained Pt NCs is denoted as 1×. The preparation of Pt NPs (5 ± 2 nm and 30 ± 4 nm), Au NPs (13 ± 2 nm), and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs (13 ± 2 nm) is described in the ESI.†

### Pt NC-induced oxidation reaction

Stock solutions of ABTS, TMB, and dopamine were prepared in deionized water. Pt NCs (50 μL, 1×) were added to a solution consisting of ABTS (100 μL, 1 mM), deionized water (350 μL), and acetate (500 μL, 100 mM; pH 2.0–6.0). The pH of acetate solution was adjusted by adding NaOH or HCl. The mixture was incubated at ambient temperature for 0–60 min. The absorption spectra of the mixture were recorded using a visible spectrophotometer. Under the same reaction conditions, TMB and dopamine were used instead of ABTS, once at a time. To study the steady-state mechanism of the Pt NC-induced oxidation reaction, variable concentrations of TMB (0.1–1 mM) was used as substrates while the concentration of the Pt NCs (0.05×) was kept constant. The zero order rate constants were obtained by linear plotting of the maximal absorbance *versus* time. Steady-state kinetic assays were carried out in 50 mM sodium acetate (pH 3.0). The kinetic parameters, $V_{max}$ and $K_m$, were calculated according to the Michaelis–Menten equation.

## 3 Results and discussion

### Synthesis and characterization of Pt NCs

The preparation of Pt NCs was done by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys under basic conditions at 37 °C for 12 h. When the concentration of Lys varied from 1 to 25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> at a fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> (10 mM), the reaction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> (approximately 0.35 mM) Lys showed the most intense fluorescence intensity at 434 nm (Fig. S1, ESI†). At the Lys concentrations of 1 and 2 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>, the formed Pt NCs exhibited weaker fluorescence as a result of the production of large-sized Pt NCs. Similar phenomena were observed when bovine serum albumin (BSA) and Lys were used to prepare BSA- and Lys-stabilized AuNCs, respectively.<sup>28,31</sup> In the concentration range of 10–25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> Lys, the fluorescence of the as-prepared Pt NCs progressively decreased with increasing Lys concentrations. This is probably due to the fact that a large amount of the precursor was consumed in the formation of nuclei, resulting in a decrease in the formation of Pt NCs. We suggest that the role of Lys is to act as the sole reducing agent. Thus, a high molar ratio of Lys to H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> facilitates the formation of Pt NCs, while a low molar ratio of Lys to H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> induces the production of Pt NPs and Pt NCs. Taken together, 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> was selected for the subsequent study. Because the size of the resulting Pt NCs was too small to be observed in standard TEM,<sup>1</sup> MALDI-TOF MS was used to determine the size of the metal NCs<sup>1</sup> and confirm the production of Pt NCs. The molecular weight of Lys was determined to be 14.3 kDa by using MADLI-TOF MS (Fig. 1A). When Lys was incubated with a solution of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>, the resulting solution was immediately detected using MALDI-TOF-MS. Fig. S2 (ESI†) shows that the formation of Au<sup>+</sup>–Lys complexes caused a shift in molecular mass. Compared to Lys and Au<sup>+</sup>–Lys complexes, the MALDI-TOF MS spectrum for Lys-stabilized Pt NCs consisted of several peaks between m/z 14 000 and 16 000 (Fig. 1B). One of these peaks appearing at 14 318 was assigned to [Lys + H]<sup>+</sup>, while other peaks with an average m/z of 15 098 corresponded to different sizes of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs. According to the difference in the m/z value between the above average peak and the Lys peak, the average number of Pt atoms in Lys-stabilized Pt NCs is 4. This signifies that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters are major products in a mixture of Pt NCs. This result also reflects that Lys may remain bound to Pt NCs during the MALDI-TOF MS process. Similar phenomena occurred during the MALDI-TOF MS analysis of protein-stabilized Au<sub>8</sub> and Au<sub>25</sub> clusters.<sup>29,31,32</sup> Differences in the molecular weights of Lys and Lys-stabilized Pt provide clear evidence for the formation of Pt NCs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the valence state of Pt NCs. The XPS spectrum of dried Pt NCs revealed a peak indicative of Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> at 71.7 eV, which differs from the reported XPS peaks for PtCl<sub>4</sub>, PtCl<sub>2</sub>, and bulk Pt, which appear at 75.0, 73.2, and 70.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 2).<sup>9</sup>

![Fig. 1 MALDI-TOF mass spectra of (A) Lys and (B) Lys-stabilized Pt NCs.](figure1.png)

![Fig. 2 X-ray photoelectron spectrum of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs. The original spectrum is in black, the fitted spectrum is in red, the Pt(I) 4f<sub>7/2</sub> spectrum is in blue, and the Pt(0) 4f<sub>7/2</sub> spectrum is in green.](figure2.png)

Moreover, the peak position of Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> for Pt NCs appears between the Pt(0) and Pt(II) peak positions. Relative to the binding energy of bulk Pt, Pt NCs showed the positive shift in the Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> peak position. This shift is attributed to the fact that the binding energy of metal clusters increases with reducing cluster size. The best fit of the data reveals that the Pt NCs contained approximately 67% Pt(I) and 33% Pt(0); there was no evidence for the presence of an oxidized precursor, Pt(II)Cl<sub>4</sub><sup>2−</sup>. This is similar to the reported composition of glutathione-protected Pt NCs, which contained 90% Pt(I) and a complementary Pt(0) content.<sup>23</sup> Lin and Tseng reported that Hg<sup>2+</sup> induces fluorescence quenching of Au<sub>25</sub> clusters through the formation of metallophilic bonds between Hg(II) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>) and Au(I) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>).<sup>28</sup> This effect could be applied to demonstrate the presence of Pt(0) in Pt NCs because Pt(0) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>) can bind to Hg(II) through metallophilic interactions.<sup>33</sup> Fig. S3 (ESI†) shows that fluorescence quenching of Pt NCs occurs when the Hg(II) ion is added, confirming the presence of Pt(0) in Pt NCs. FT-IR was used to examine the interactions between Lys and Pt NCs. The FT-IR spectrum of Lys contains two major bands at 1664 and 1543 cm<sup>−1</sup>, which correspond to amide I (C=O stretching) and amide II (N–H bending coupled with C–N stretching) bonds, respectively (curve a in Fig. S4, ESI†). By contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs shows less intense absorptions at 1664 and 1543 cm<sup>−1</sup>, suggesting that there are fewer amide I and II bonds present because the occurrence of the Lys secondary structure decreased (curve b in Fig. S4, ESI†). Comparing the Lys and Lys-stabilized Pt NC spectra reveals that the Lys amide I and II bonds are blue-shifted in the complex, suggesting that Pt NCs interact with the Lys peptide bonds. Sennuga *et al.* reported that the interaction between Pt NPs and apoferritin results in similar shifts in the amide I and II absorption bands.<sup>34</sup>

### Optical properties and stability of Pt NCs

In a solution, H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> has an absorbance maximum at 260 nm (Fig 3A), which results from a ligand-to-metal charge transfer within the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> ions.<sup>35</sup> Following the addition of Lys to a solution of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> ions, the absorbance intensity at 260 nm decreases (Fig. 3A), whereas the fluorescence gradually increases (Fig. 3B). After incubating overnight at 37 °C, the fluorescence intensity of a mixture of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> complex ions and Lys was remarkably larger than that of Lys (Fig. S5, ESI†). These results suggest that the observed blue emission arises from the Pt NCs, and not from Lys. Thus, a mixture of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> complex ions and Lys progressively converted to Pt NCs over time. The maximal emission shifts to longer wavelengths when the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 500 nm, suggesting that the as-prepared solution contains a mixture of Pt NCs with different particle sizes (Fig. 3C). When a long pass filter (475 nm cut-off filter) was used to block scattering from the excitation wavelength, we still observed that the maximal emission shifted from 510 to 556 nm by changing the excitation wavelength from 425 to 465 nm (Fig. S6, ESI†). We rule out that excitation-dependent change in spectra is due to scattering from Lys and Pt NCs. Thus, excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior arises from different sized NCs in the as-prepared solution. Kawasaki *et al.* found that the maximal fluorescence emission wavelength for ligand-free Pt NCs increases when the excitation wavelength increases.<sup>21</sup> The most intense fluorescence emission occurs at 434 nm when the solution was excited at 370 nm, indicating that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters are dominant in a mixture of Pt NCs, according to the spherical Jellium model:<sup>36</sup>

$$E_{Fermi}/N^{1/3} \text{ (} E_{Fermi} = 4.28 \text{ eV for Pt).}$$

![Fig. 3 (A) Absorption and (B) fluorescence spectra obtained from the synthesis of Pt NCs at different growth times. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Pt NCs with excitation of different wavelengths. (D) Fluorescence lifetime decay of Pt NCs.](figure3.png)

A tetrahedral geometry provides the lowest energy structure for Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters.<sup>37</sup> Using quinine sulfate as the reference, we estimated that the Pt NCs provide a quantum yield of 8% (Fig. S7, ESI†). Numerically fitting the fluorescence at 434 nm (excitation at 370 nm) revealed a fluorescence lifetime of 3.0 ns for the blue-emitting Pt NCs (Fig. 3D). This short lifetime may originate from singlet transitions between the filled d- and sp-conduction bands. To demonstrate the formation of different sized Pt NCs, lifetime values of green-emitting Pt NCs were obtained by numerically fitting the fluorescence at 520 nm (excitation at 485 nm). Fig. S8 (ESI†) shows that Pt NCs exhibited two lifetimes of 5.4 (73%) and 36.1 ns (27%). We tested the stability of Pt NCs by monitoring their fluorescence in a 50 mM phosphate solution (pH 7.0) containing 0–400 mM NaCl (Fig. S9, ESI†). The fluorescence remained constant for the range of test conditions, indicating that Pt NCs have potential for molecular sensing under physiological conditions.

### Pt NCs mimic oxidase activity

HRP catalyzes the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of ABTS and TMB. These dyes have applications in bioassay, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and biosensing applications. Recently, NP-based materials, including Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Au NPs, were used in place of HRP because they offer greater resistance to denaturation and protease digestion and are simpler to prepare and store.<sup>11,38,39</sup> However, the peroxidase-catalyzed reactions require H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> as an electron acceptor. CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs, Pt nanodot-coated Au nanorods, and PdPt alloy nanodot-coated Au nanorods exhibit oxidase-like activity in the catalytic O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of ABTS and TMB.<sup>40–42</sup> Because the template-based direct synthesis of metal NCs does not require the use of stabilizing ligands, metal clusters located in templates can provide non-block, active surfaces for catalyzing chemical reactions.<sup>1</sup> We used Lys as a template for synthesizing Pt NCs and investigated whether Lys-stabilized Pt NCs catalyze the oxidation of ABTS and TMB. Lys-stabilized Pt NCs were incubated with ABTS in 50 mM acetate solution at pH 2.0–6.0. Fig. 4A and B show the time-dependent absorbance at 414 nm and color changes of 0.1 mM ABTS following the addition of 0.05× Lys-stabilized Pt NCs to an ABTS solution with a pH ranging from 2.0 to 6.0. Fig. S10 (ESI†) shows their corresponding visible spectra. In the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, the maximal catalytic rate for Pt NC-catalyzed oxidation of ABTS was at pH 2.0. In the absence of Pt NCs, no oxidation of ABTS is apparent over the pH range of 2.0 to 6.0 (Fig. S11, ESI†). Under the same concentration of Pt NCs, 0.1 mM TMB underwent Pt NC-induced oxidation and color change at acidic pH levels (Fig. 4B and S12, ESI†). By monitoring the change in absorbance at 650 nm, the maximal catalytic rate for Pt NC-catalyzed one electron oxidation of TMB was at pH 3.0. This result is inconsistent with our prediction that the most rapid oxidation of TMB occurs at pH 2.0 in the presence of Pt NCs. This is probably attributed to the fact that Pt NCs catalyze two electron oxidation of TMB at pH 2.0, generating the oxidized TMB with an absorbance maximum at 450 nm.<sup>43</sup> We used dopamine to verify the Pt NCs' oxidation properties because of its resistance to oxidation. Adding 0.05× Pt NCs to a solution of 0.1 mM dopamine at pH 7.0 caused the solution to change from colorless to orange (Fig. 4B) and the characteristic absorption bands in the UV-visible spectrum to emerge (Fig. S13, ESI†). These changes are characteristic of dopamine conversion to aminochrome, a major oxidation product.<sup>44</sup> In the absence of Pt NCs, the UV-visible spectrum and color of the dopamine solution remain almost constant, even after days of incubation. Taken together, these results clearly demonstrate the intrinsic oxidase-like activity of Pt NCs. Fig. 4C shows that, at a pH of 4.0, the catalytic activity of Pt NCs toward the oxidation of TMB follows a Michaelis–Menten mechanism. The Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and maximal velocity ($V_{max}$) were determined to be 0.63 mM and 2.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>, respectively, by using the Michaelis–Menten equation. Compared to HRP- and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NP-catalyzed oxidation of TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>,<sup>38</sup> Pt NCs provide a relatively small value of $K_m$ (high affinity for the substrate), and a relatively large value for $V_{max}$. This result clearly indicates that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters provide higher catalyst efficiency even in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. Additionally, in comparison with oxidase-like activity of CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs (size ≈ 5–100 nm, $K_m$ = 0.8–3.8 mM, $V_{max}$ = 0.3–0.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>),<sup>40</sup> Pt NCs show a lower $K_m$ value and a higher $V_{max}$ value than CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs. In addition to examining Pt NCs, we investigated whether HRP, Lys, Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs (13 ± 2 nm), Au NPs (13 ± 2 nm), Pt NPs (30 ± 4 nm), and Pt NPs (5 ± 1 nm) catalyze the oxidation of ABTS in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. However, Fig. 4D reveals that only Pt NCs exhibited high performance in the catalytic oxidation of ABTS.

Fig. 4 (A) Effect of solution pH on the catalytic rate of Pt NCs. 0.05× Pt NCs were incubated with a solution containing 0.1 mM ABTS and 50 mM sodium acetate. (B) Photographs of the colored product obtained from the reaction of 0.05× Pt NCs with 0.1 mM ABTS, 0.1 mM TMB, and 0.1 mM dopamine at pH 2.0, 3.0, and 7.0, respectively. (C) Double reciprocal plot of activity of Pt NCs with the constant concentration of Pt NCs. The velocity ($v$) obtained from the incubation of Pt NCs with different concentrations of TMB at pH 4.0. (D) The intensity of the absorbance at 414 nm of oxidized ABTS obtained in the absence (a) and presence of (b) Lys, (c) HRP, (d) 13 nm diameter Au NPs, (e) 13 nm diameter Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs, (f) 30 nm diameter Pt NPs, (g) 5 nm diameter Pt NPs, and (h) Pt NCs. ABTS (0.1 mM, pH 2.0) was incubated with nanomaterial/protein at ambient temperature for 10 min. The concentration of each nanomaterial and protein was fixed at 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>.

The catalytic oxidation of ABTS by Pt-based nanomaterials follows the trend Pt NCs > Pt NPs (5 nm) > Pt NPs (30 nm), suggesting that the catalytic activity of Pt-based nanomaterials is highly dependent on the particle size. This is attributed to the fact that Pt NCs have a high surface area to volume ratio, thus providing more active sites for the catalysis of ABTS. This result also suggests that the use of a template did not block the active surfaces of metal clusters during catalysis. Previous studies have demonstrated that Pt NCs can efficiently catalyze the reduction of oxygen to water through a four-electron reduction reaction in electrocatalytic processes.<sup>9,45</sup> He *et al.* coated Au nanorods with Pt nanodots and used them as oxidase mimetics for the catalytic oxidation of TMB through a four-electron process.<sup>41</sup> They suggested that Au–Pt nanostructures, dissolved O<sub>2</sub>, and TMB served as a catalyst, an electron acceptor, and an electron donor, respectively. Fig. 5 shows that the as-prepared Pt NCs cannot catalyze the oxidation of ABTS under N<sub>2</sub>-saturated conditions, which indicates the importance of O<sub>2</sub> in the oxidation process. Because Pt NCs could reduce O<sub>2</sub> to H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> through a two-electron reduction process [O<sub>2</sub>(g) + 2H<sup>+</sup>(aq) + 2e<sup>−</sup> → H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>(aq)] and then catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of the organic substrate, catalase converting H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> into water was used to exclude this possibility. Because the absorption spectrum of oxidized ABTS overlaps that of catalase, TMB was used instead of ABTS. Fig. S14 (ESI†) shows that catalase rarely inhibited the Pt NC-catalyzed O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of TMB. According to previous studies and our present findings, we propose that the possible mechanism of Pt NC-catalyzed O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of organic substrates involves a four-electron reduction process [O<sub>2</sub>(g) + 4H<sup>+</sup>(aq) + 4e<sup>−</sup> → 2H<sub>2</sub>O(l)]. Moreover, dissolved O<sub>2</sub> and ABTS act as an electron acceptor and an electron donor, respectively. These findings motivated us to apply Pt NCs to the oxidation of pollutants in environmental water. Following the addition of Pt NCs, the 665 nm maximal absorbance of methylene blue in lake water samples gradually decreased (Fig. S15, ESI†). Pt NCs degrade methylene blue in a complex matrix in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. By contrast, previous studies have reported that high concentrations of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> are required for metal NP-induced degradation of environmental pollutants.<sup>46</sup>

### Conclusions
This study has reported a straightforward one-pot approach to prepare blue-emitting and oxidase-like Pt NCs using Lys as a template at a pH of 12.0. This result is helpful to realize that protein can act as unique template for synthesizing not only Au and Ag NCs, but also Pt NCs. Lys-stabilized Pt NCs catalyze the O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of ABTS, TMB, and dopamine through a four-electron reduction process. We demonstrated the practical applicability of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs in degrading methylene blue in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. We found that the fluorescence quenching of Pt NCs occurs in the presence of Hg(II) and Cu(II), and this is a subject requiring further investigation.

data: 1. **Experiment Identification**: The text explicitly reports kinetic parameters ($K_m$ and $V_{max}$) for the oxidation of TMB by Pt NCs in Section 3 ('Pt NCs mimic oxidase activity'). The text states: 'The Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and maximal velocity ($V_{max}$) were determined to be 0.63 mM and 2.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>, respectively'. This meets the mandatory inclusion criteria. Other experiments (ABTS, dopamine) describe activity but do not report calculated $K_m$ or $V_{max}$ values, so they are excluded. 2. **Activity Determination**: The reaction involves the oxidation of TMB using dissolved $O_2$ without added $H_2O_2$, which defines 'oxidase' activity. 3. **Formula Extraction**: The material is identified as 'Pt NCs' or 'Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters'. The inorganic formula is 'Pt'. 4. **Surface Extraction**: The clusters are 'Lys-stabilized' and synthesized using 'lysozyme (Lys) as a template'. The text confirms Lys remains bound ('Lys may remain bound to Pt NCs'). Thus, 'Lys' is the surface component. 5. **Kinetic Parameters**: $K_m$ = '0.63', unit 'mM'. $V_{max}$ = '2.7', unit 'μM s<sup>−1</sup>'. 6. **Experimental Conditions**: pH is explicitly stated as '4.0' for the kinetic plot. Temperature is described as 'ambient temperature' in the general method, but no numeric value is given for the specific kinetic run, so it is null. Catalyst concentration is '0.05×' (a dilution factor of the stock), which is the explicit value reported for the assay. Substrate range is '0.1–1 mM' (TMB). 7. **Size/Structure**: The text states the size is 'too small to be observed in standard TEM' and relies on MALDI-TOF for cluster size (Pt4). No explicit TEM/SEM dimensions (length/width/depth) are reported for the final nanozyme, so these are null. Syngony is not explicitly stated for the final cluster in the context of the experiment (though tetrahedral geometry is mentioned for Pt4, it's a theoretical model, not an experimental crystal system report like 'cubic' from XRD).

data:
Nanoplasmonics DOI: 10.1002/anie.201105121 # Catalytic Gold Nanoparticles for Nanoplasmonic Detection of DNA Hybridization** Xiaoxue Zheng, Qing Liu, Chao Jing, Yang Li, Di Li,* Weijie Luo, Yanqin Wen, Yao He, Qing Huang, Yi-Tao Long, and Chunhai Fan* Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) possess many attractive optical and electronic properties that have proven to be of high utility in biomedical applications.[1] In particular, biomolecular detection methods have been developed by exploiting surface plasmon resonance (SPR),[2] Raman,[3] fluorescence,[4] and conductance[5] properties of AuNPs. However, there is another largely ignored region, that is, the catalytic activity of AuNPs.[6] Although bulk gold is generally considered to be chemically inert, substrate-supported AuNPs have been known to possess surprisingly high catalytic activities toward the oxidation of CO or NO.[7] Recently, colloidal AuNPs were found to exhibit glucose oxidase (GOx)-like activity.[8, 9] Our previous work revealed that this enzyme-like activity of AuNPs was extremely sensitive to surface properties, which led to the design of a self-limiting growth system.[9] We speculate that these unprecedented findings open a new avenue toward biological applications with catalytic AuNPs. AuNPs interact with biomolecules in various ways, based on which hybrid nanobiomaterials with synergetic properties and functions have been developed.[10] DNA–AuNP conjugates that rely on gold–sulfur chemistry is an elegant example,[11] which has become an important building block for a broad spectrum of bioassays,[12] nanostructures, and nanodevices.[13] Alternatively, noncovalent interactions between as-prepared AuNPs and DNA strands have been actively exploited to detect DNA hybridization and aptamer binding reactions.[14] As unstructured, single-stranded (ss-) DNA noncovalently binds to as-prepared AuNPs much more rapidly and strongly than structured, double-stranded (ds-) DNA,[15] DNA hybridization can effectively tune the stability of AuNPs in a high-concentration salt solution. Herein, we aim to amplify these noncovalent interactions by using catalytic AuNPs, and develop a new nanoplasmonic probe for biomolecular detection. As-prepared AuNPs can catalytically oxidize glucose in the presence of O2, producing gluconic acid and H2O2 [Scheme 1, Eq. (1)]:[8] $$ \text{glucose} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{AuNPs}} \text{gluconic acid} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \quad (1) $$ ![Scheme 1](Scheme_1_Illustration.png) **Scheme 1.** Illustration of the GOx-like catalytic activity of AuNPs regulated by DNA hybridization, which can be either amplified by HRP-cascaded color or chemiluminescence variations (path a) or lead to nanoplasmonic changes owing to size enlargement (path b). Orange strand = target, green strand = adsorption probe. When this catalytic reaction is coupled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-based enzymatic catalysis, this cascade reaction results in a characteristic blue color owing to the oxidation of a cosubstrate of ABTS2− (ABTS = 2,2’-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) by HRP [Eq. (2); Supporting Information, Figure S1]: $$ \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{ABTS}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\text{HRP}} \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{ABTS}^{\bullet-} \quad (2) $$ Given that the GOx-like activity of as-prepared AuNPs is extremely sensitive to surface passivation,[9] we attempted to investigate variation of the catalytic activity of AuNPs upon interaction with ss-DNA (adsorption probe **1**) and ds-DNA (probe **1**/target **3** (Figure 1A). When **1** was first hybridized with the fully complementary DNA **3** to form a duplex and then subjected to the cascade reaction, we observed the appearance of the characteristic blue color. Interestingly, if AuNPs were first mixed with ss-DNA followed by the HRP cascade reaction, the color change was largely attenuated, suggesting an effective suppression of the cascade reaction by ss-DNA. As AuNPs were removed by ultracentrifugation before the addition of HRP, this suppression should solely arise from the binding of ss-DNA to the surface of AuNPs. This remarkable difference reflects the different adsorption ability of ss- and ds-DNA to as-prepared AuNPs, a phenomenon consistent with previous observations in salt-stability-based colorimetric assays.[15] In a control experiment, we challenged AuNPs with a mixture of probe **1** and a non-cognate DNA strand **4**, the solution was also less intensively blue as in the case of probe **1** alone, suggesting that the sequence-specific formation of DNA duplex is critical for regulating the catalysis of AuNPs. In this control, the absorption intensity was even lower than that of probe **1** alone. This is because the additional non-cognate ss-DNA **4** further increased the coverage of ss-DNA on the surface of AuNPs. Therefore, ss-DNA can adsorb strongly to the surface of AuNPs, resulting in deactivated catalysis, while ds-DNA binds weakly to AuNPs and only slightly perturbs the catalytic activity of AuNPs. ![Figure 1](Figure_1_Kinetics.png) **Figure 1.** A) Time-dependent changes to absorbance of ABTS•− upon treating bare AuNPs (5 nm) with ds-DNA (**1**/**3**, 1 μM each), or ss-DNA alone (**1**, 1 μM), or mixture of ss-DNA (**1** and **4**, 1 μM each) for 5 min. B) Kinetics study of bare AuNPs (▲), ss-DNA–AuNPs (●), and ds-DNA–AuNPs (■). The reaction rate V is defined as the amount of generated of gluconic acid in a fixed reaction time of 30 min. The concentration of AuNPs was 15 nM and the concentrations of ss- and ds-DNA were 0.75 μM. We further performed kinetic studies by using the Michaelis–Menten model to provide a quantitative measurement of this hybridization-regulated catalytic reaction. Figure 1 B illustrates the double-reciprocal plots for the as-prepared AuNPs, ss-DNA–AuNPs, and ds-DNA–AuNPs. AuNPs were centrifuged to prevent possible influence of the color of AuNPs to the colorimetric reaction. Significantly, the values for the Michaelis–Menten constant ($K_m$) and the catalytic constant ($k_{cat}$) of ss-DNA–AuNPs was about three times larger and about four times lower than that of as-prepared AuNPs, respectively (Table 1). The decrease in both thermodynamic affinity (increased $K_m$) and kinetics (decreased $k_{cat}$) implies that the catalysis of AuNPs toward glucose is suppressed by binding with ss-DNA. In contrast, the $K_m$ of ds-DNA–AuNPs was only slightly increased as compared to as-prepared AuNPs, and the corresponding $k_{cat}$ was decreased by about 25%, suggesting that the activity of as-prepared AuNPs was only slightly perturbed by ds-DNA. **Table 1:** Kinetic parameters of as-prepared 13 nm AuNPs, ss-DNA–AuNPs, and ds-DNA–AuNPs with glucose substrate.[a]
[AuNP] [nmol L−1] Km [mmol L−1] Vmax [μL mol s−1] kcat [s−1] kcat/Km [mM−1 s−1]
bare AuNPs 15 4.73 ± 0.37 0.68 ± 0.03 47.33 ± 2.00 9.99
ss-DNA–AuNPs 15 17.67 ± 2.07 0.18 ± 0.04 12.00 ± 2.00 0.68
ds-DNA–AuNPs 15 6.98 ± 0.69 0.53 ± 0.04 35.33 ± 2.67 5.06
[a] $K_m$ = Michaelis–Menten constant, $k_{cat}$ = catalytic constant, $V_{max}$ = maximum reaction rate. Given that DNA hybridization can specifically switch on the catalytic activity of AuNPs, we were motivated to design a catalytic AuNP-based strategy for DNA analysis (Scheme 1, path a). We reason that the catalytic activity of AuNPs, as well as its coupled cascade amplification, can be finely regulated by DNA hybridization, providing a quantitative measurement for target DNA. The HRP-based colorimetric or chemiluminescent (CL) detection led to limit of detection (LOD) of 14 nM and 0.75 nM, respectively (Supporting Information, Figure S2, S3). This catalytic AuNP-based strategy can be employed to detect microRNAs (miRNAs; Supporting Information, Figure S4), a class of short (19–24 bases), endogenous non-coding RNAs with promising applications in clinical diagnostics.[16, 17] By using probe DNA **2** that was complementary to hsa-let-7e, which is a target belonging to the let-7 miRNA family that has been known to be associated with tumors,[18, 19] we found that hsa-let-7e could be quantified by using the catalytic AuNP-based colorimetric measurements (Supporting Information, Figure S4A), leading to a LOD of 8 nM ($> 3\sigma$). Significantly, we found that the specific hsa-let-7e exhibited significantly higher color intensity than all other miRNAs (Supporting Information, Figure S4B). The same strategy was adaptable to aptamer-based assays, as aptamers often undergo significant structural variations from unstructured random coils to rigid structures upon target binding.[14e, 20, 21] A K+-specific G quartet **7** was employed to demonstrate the possibility of using catalytic AuNPs in aptamer-based assays (Supporting Information, Figure S5). Similar to DNA hybridization, we found that this binding process recovered the catalytic activity of AuNPs, as manifested by the intensified color change in the presence of K+. This assay could detect as low as 0.5 mM of K+ with high selectivity over other monovalent cations (Li+, Na+, Rb+, NH4+). Apart from the cascade reaction with HRP, the catalytic AuNPs can be coupled with the AuNP-mediated seed growth in the presence of HAuCl4 (Scheme 1, path b). The H2O2 generated in situ reduces HAuCl4 to Au0, which is deposited on the surface of AuNP seed, resulting in gradual size enlargement of AuNPs.[9] AuNP-seeded growth significantly alters the localized SPR (or nanoplasmonic) properties of AuNPs, which has been exploited to develop high-sensitivity bioassays and cellular/tissue imaging.[22] AuNPs possess intense SPR-enhanced light scattering,[23] and the true color of the light scattered from a single AuNP can be visually detected by using dark-field illumination. Dark-field microscopy (DFM) provides a powerful means to directly image size and shape variations of AuNPs in real time and at the single nanoparticle level.[24] The advantage of DFM to track single plasmonic NPs facilitates its use as labels in bioassays.[25] Particularly, multiplex targets detection and in vivo imaging using plasmonic NPs of different colors are of great promise for applications.[26] Simultaneously recorded Rayleigh scattering spectra also provides a mechanistic understanding of the AuNP growth system. In our DFM setup, the optical resolution allows the tracking of single AuNPs with a minimum size of 50 nm in diameter. As shown in Figure 2 A1, bare AuNPs of 50 nm that were deposited on silanized glass slides exhibited green scattering light with the maximum wavelength at about 570 nm. It is worth noting that, while the majority of AuNPs on a slide were of a green color, there were also AuNPs of other colors, reflecting the heterogeneous size distribution of chemically synthesized AuNPs. Importantly, since the positions of AuNPs were fixed on silanized slides, it is possible to track single AuNP with DFM and obtain reliable imaging information. Upon the addition of glucose and HAuCl4, which activates the growth process, a gradual color change that resulted in red scattering light was observed after 25 min, with the scattering peak intensity enhanced and red-shifted to 675 nm (Figure 2 A2). This result suggests that bare AuNPs are enlarged during the coupled reaction (Scheme 1, path b), which coincides well with our observations in solution-phase studies.[9] The presence of ss-DNA (probe **1**) largely suppressed the growth of AuNPs (Figure 2 B1, B2), with minimum change in both DFM imaging and scattering spectra, which further confirms that ss-DNA adsorbs on AuNPs and blocks its catalytic activity. Significantly, hybridization of the probe **1** with target **3** could switch on the growth of AuNPs. DFM clearly showed that the color and brightness change of ds-DNA–AuNPs after the coupled growth reaction (Figure 2 C1, C2). While the peak shift and intensity are smaller than that of as-prepared bare AuNPs, they are significantly larger than that of ss-DNA–AuNPs, confirming that ds-DNA adsorbs on AuNPs with much weaker affinity than ss-DNA. Meanwhile, DFM images of probe **1** with single-base mismatched DNA samples **5** exhibited a slight color variation and moderate brightness change after the enlargement reaction (Figure 2 D1, D2), implying that the catalysis-based nanoplasmonic Au nanoprobe can identify DNA targets with high specificity. The DNA regulated growth of AuNPs was also monitored by SEM characterizations with similar conclusion (Supporting Information, Figure S6). ![Figure 2](Figure_2_DFM.png) **Figure 2.** Left: Dark-field microscopy (DFM) images of large AuNPs (50 nm in diameter) before (1) and after (2) the 25 min glucose-induced enlargement process. A) Bare AuNPs, B) ss-DNA–AuNPs, C) ds-DNA–AuNPs, D) ds–DNA with 1-base mismatches interacting with AuNPs. Right: The light scattering spectra corresponding to individual nanoparticle marked with the circle in the DFM images. $I_{sc}$ = scattering intensity. See the text for further details. We found that the growth rate of AuNPs with different sizes was regulated differentially with ss-DNA. When ss-DNA was present, larger AuNPs (orange-colored dots) typically grew faster than smaller ones (green-colored dots; Supporting Information, Figure S7). Since our previous solution-phase study has revealed that AuNPs with smaller size possessed higher catalytic activity,[9] this difference in growth rates should arise from the surface coverage of ss-DNA on AuNPs. Under the same conditions, larger AuNPs has larger surface areas and are less covered by ss-DNA; thereby, more unoccupied surface could proceed to catalyze glucose to produce larger NPs. Therefore, we could in principle employ smaller AuNPs to increase the difference in nanoplasmonic signals in response to ss- and ds-DNA. In principle, smaller AuNPs possess higher catalytic activity and can be more easily covered by ss-DNA than larger ones. To test this hypothesis, we further employed AuNPs of 12 nm in diameter (Supporting Information, Figure S8). These small AuNPs were barely visible under DFM owing to the limited optical resolution of our system. The growth processes were recorded in real time (see the video in the Supporting Information). Interestingly, colored dots rapidly appeared within only 10 minutes upon addition of glucose and HAuCl4. After 30 minutes of enlargement, hundreds of red dots with high brightness levels appeared in the view (Supporting Information, Figure S8A). This result suggests that small AuNPs can rapidly grow into larger AuNPs with sizes exceeding 50 nm. Obviously, this signal-on process with nearly zero background is more sensitive than that with 50 nm AuNPs, which was then employed to differentiate ss- and ds-DNA. Indeed, the critical time for the appearance of colored dots ($t_{50}$, defined by a greater than 50% increase in the number of dots) was delayed from 10 min for the as-prepared AuNPs to 30 min for ss-DNA–AuNPs (Supporting Information, Figure S8B). The $t_{50}$ value for ds-DNA–AuNPs was 18 minutes, although the color change from green-to-orange was much slower than for as-prepared AuNPs. We have demonstrated that the GOx-like catalytic activity of AuNPs can be finely regulated by DNA hybridization, and that these catalytic AuNPs can be employed as a nanoprobe for a variety of biomolecular detections. This new strategy has several unprecedented advantages. First, it exploits the catalytic rather than optical or electric properties of AuNPs. Therefore, it could be easily coupled with various enzymatic cascade reactions to amply hybridization signals. Furthermore, the use of more active Au nanomaterials, for example, hollow Au nanocages with more active atoms at the surface,[27] is expected to improve the sensitivity of this system. Second, this assay method avoids labeled DNA probes, which simplifies the system and lowers the assay cost. Third, this strategy could potentially be used in any systems that could incorporate catalytic AuNPs. As we have demonstrated its applicability in aptamer-based assays, it should in principle be a generic platform for the detection of any small molecules, proteins or even cell targets that have specific aptamers. The mechanism for the observed remarkable difference in adsorption ability of DNA to AuNPs arises primarily from different accessibility of AuNPs to nitrogen-containing nucleotides in ss- and ds-DNA. Moreover, higher surface charge density and rigidity of ds-DNA (or aptamers) also make it more difficult to bind to AuNPs than ss-DNA. Significantly, the adsorption of nucleotides on Au atoms inhibits their catalytic activity, resulting in the DNA hybridization-regulated catalysis of AuNPs. Our previous study has revealed that the size, shape, and catalytic activity of AuNPs could be simultaneously controlled to result in a self-limiting system.[9] This implies that there is plenty of room to further optimize the system by finely tuning DNA–AuNPs interactions to minimize the background and amply the signal. It is worthwhile to point out that, as AuNP-based catalysis is a complicated reaction with multiple active sites for glucose oxidation and possible substrate suppression, the classic Michaelis–Menten equation cannot precisely describe this reaction. Furthermore, the rate of catalysis would decrease with the adsorption of glucose and its product gluconic acid owing to reduced access to the NP surface, which also does not fit the Michaelis–Menten equation. This substrate suppression is, nevertheless, at a much smaller level than DNA adsorption based suppression. However, this equation is still employed as it provides a simple measurement for comparison of kinetics. Our DFM study opens a new avenue for the use of catalytic AuNPs as a nanoplasmonic probe for various types of biomolecular recognition. While our present system is limited by heterogeneous size distribution of chemically synthesized AuNPs, the recently developed methods for the synthesis of monodispersed AuNPs provide a route to increase the detection sensitivity.[28] Also interestingly, this catalysis-based nanoplasmonic Au probe can be coupled with DNA nanotechnology-based solution-phase DNA chips[29] to amplify the hybridization signal. We also note that citrate-capped AuNPs may not be directly (that is, without prior sample separation) applied in biological fluids owing to strong protein adsorption. However, it is possible to use AuNPs with stronger ligands that are not so surface sensitive (for example hydroxylamine-capped AuNPs) to realize bioanalysis in biological fluids. Furthermore, AuNPs are known to possess high cellular uptake ability in various cell types without apparent cell toxicity. Therefore, it is possible to design a system for tracking cellular events in real time by using this catalytic nanoplasmonic probe.[26b] The single NP tracking ability of DFM could provide invaluable information occurring on the NP surface.[30] Received: July 21, 2011 Revised: August 16, 2011 Published online: October 13, 2011 Supporting information for this article is available on the WWW under http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/anie.201105121.

data:
- **Experiment 1 (bare AuNPs)**: Source: Table 1. Key Values: Formula="Au", Activity="glucose oxidase" (assay oxidizes glucose), Km="4.73 ± 0.37", Km_unit="mmol L⁻¹", Vmax="0.68 ± 0.03", Vmax_unit="μL mol s⁻¹", ccat_value="15", ccat_unit="nmol L⁻¹". Nulls: Temperature, pH, Size (Table refers to 13 nm, but text mentions 5 nm in Fig 1 caption; Table 1 header says "13 nm", so Length/Width/Depth="13", unit="nm"), Surface (citrate is synthesis agent, not explicitly part of final identity name in table row, though implied; strict rule says exclude synthesis-only agents unless part of identity name. Table row says "bare AuNPs", so Surface=null).
- **Experiment 2 (ss-DNA–AuNPs)**: Source: Table 1. Key Values: Formula="Au", Activity="glucose oxidase", Km="17.67 ± 2.07", Km_unit="mmol L⁻¹", Vmax="0.18 ± 0.04", Vmax_unit="μL mol s⁻¹", ccat_value="15", ccat_unit="nmol L⁻¹", Surface="ss-DNA" (explicitly part of the material name "ss-DNA–AuNPs"). Nulls: Temperature, pH, Size (assumed same 13 nm as base, but strictly text says "13 nm AuNPs" in table header, so Length/Width/Depth="13", unit="nm").
- **Experiment 3 (ds-DNA–AuNPs)**: Source: Table 1. Key Values: Formula="Au", Activity="glucose oxidase", Km="6.98 ± 0.69", Km_unit="mmol L⁻¹", Vmax="0.53 ± 0.04", Vmax_unit="μL mol s⁻¹", ccat_value="15", ccat_unit="nmol L⁻¹", Surface="ds-DNA" (explicitly part of the material name "ds-DNA–AuNPs"). Nulls: Temperature, pH, Size (Length/Width/Depth="13", unit="nm").
- **Note on Size**: Table 1 header explicitly states "Kinetic parameters of as-prepared 13 nm AuNPs...". Figure 1 caption mentions "bare AuNPs (5 nm)". The kinetic data in Table 1 is explicitly linked to the 13 nm particles in the table header. I will use 13 nm for the size fields corresponding to the kinetic data.
- **Note on Activity**: The reaction is glucose + O2 -> gluconic acid + H2O2. This is glucose oxidase activity.
- **Note on Reaction Type**: Substrate is glucose. Co-substrate O2 is a gas and excluded. Reaction type = "glucose".

# One-step solvothermal synthesis of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite as magnetically recyclable mimetic peroxidase

Zhonghua Wang*, Ming Chen, Jinxia Shu, Yang Li

Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, PR China

### ARTICLE INFO

**Article history:**
Received 21 February 2016
Received in revised form 10 April 2016
Accepted 26 April 2016
Available online 28 April 2016

**Keywords:**
One-step method
Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite
Mimetic peroxidase
H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> detection
Magnetic recyclability and reusability

### ABSTRACT

Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was prepared by a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method and characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS and XPS techniques. The Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite showed peroxidase-like activity, it could catalyze the oxidation of typical peroxidase substrates, o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>). A simple colorimetric method for the detection of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> could be established based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. In addition, the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite displayed good separation ability, stability and reusability, which could be easily separated from the reaction system by an external magnet and reused for the next round of reaction without significant decrease of its catalytic activity. We expect that the as-prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite may have the potential to replace natural peroxidase in the fields of catalysis, green chemistry, biosensors, environmental monitoring and protection.

© 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

## 1. Introduction

Natural enzymes, due to their high efficiency and mild reaction conditions, have been extensively studied and widely used in the field of medicine, chemical industry, food processing and agriculture [1,2], environmental monitoring and protection [3,4]. However, natural enzymes have their own intrinsic drawbacks and disadvantages, such as degradation by protease, denaturation under strong acidic and alkaline conditions, and at high temperature conditions, as well as the requirement of specials storage conditions [5,6]. Moreover, the preparation, purification and storage of natural enzymes are usually expensive and time-consuming [7,8]. Therefore, the synthesis of artificial enzyme mimics has long been a major concern of chemists and biochemists [9–13].

Recently, Gao et al. has reported that Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles possess intrinsic enzyme activity similar to that of natural peroxidases such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which opens the door to the development of nanoscaled inorganic materials in biochemical field [14]. Since this report, a number of nanoscaled metal oxides, metals and metal alloys, such as Co<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>-nanorods [15], VO<sub>2</sub> nanobelts [16], Ag<sub>2</sub>O nanoparticles [6], Cu nanoclusters [17], Au nanoparticles [18], Au@Pt nanostructures [19] and Au@PdPt alloy nanorods [20], have been investigated and found to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

Noble metal nanoparticles, such as Pt, Au and Ag nanoparticles, have attracted intensive research interest because of their unique electronic structures and excellent catalytic properties [21–23]. However, the use of noble metals is limited to some degree due to their high cost and limited supply. Compared with noble metals such as Au and Ag, Cu is a much cheap metal [24]. In the past years, much attention has been paid to prepare nanoscaled Cu, Cu<sub>2</sub>O, CuO and Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composites and to investigate their application in the field of catalysis [25,26] and environmental protection [24,25,27]. For example, Liu and co-workers fabricated porous CuO micro-/nanostructures by a two-step route and probed their peroxidase-like activity [28]. Zhou and co-workers developed a two-step solvothermal method to prepare flower-like Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposites and found that some of the Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposites exhibited higher photocatalytic performance than pure Cu<sub>2</sub>O [29]. Ai and co-workers developed an interfacial hydrothermal method for the preparation of Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O core–shell composite microspheres and investigated their visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity [30]. Kou and co-workers synthesized porous Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanoparticles through a two-step strategy involving dealloying and surface oxidation, and investigated the photocatalytic performance and adsorption behavior of the as-prepared nanoparticles [24,25]. In order to overcome the problem of recycling Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanoparticles from the reaction system, Wang and co-workers introduced magnetic Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> into the Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanoparticles and prepared magnetically recyclable Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu<sub>2</sub>O@Cu nanocomposites by a two-step method [31]. However, some of the materials are synthesized through complicated and multi-step procedures, and some need toxic organic solvent, or the synthesized materials are difficult to be recycled. Therefore, it is still necessary for material researchers to find a simple and convenient method to prepare Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite with easily recyclable properties. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, the peroxidase-like activity of Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite has not yet been systematically studied, although there are some publications about the peroxidase-like activity of Cu and CuO [17,28,32,33].

In this study, we reported a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method for the preparation of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite, and investigated the peroxidase-like activity of the nanocomposite with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) as model peroxidase substrates. It was demonstrated that the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, as it could catalyze the oxidation of TMB and OPDA to produce colored products in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>). Additionally, the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite can be easily magnetically recycled and reused without significant loss of the catalytic activity, indicating that the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite has the potential for the replacement of natural peroxidases in practical applications.

## 2. Experimental

### 2.1. Materials

Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO<sub>4</sub>·5H<sub>2</sub>O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl<sub>3</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc·3H<sub>2</sub>O), ethylene glycol (EG), o-phenylenediamine (OPDA), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, 30 wt%) were purchased from Chendu Kelong Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.

### 2.2. Preparation of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite

The Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was prepared by a facile one-step solvothermal method. Typically, 1 mmol CuSO<sub>4</sub>·5H<sub>2</sub>O (0.25 g), 2 mmol FeCl<sub>3</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O (0.54 g) and 20 mmol NaAc·3H<sub>2</sub>O (2.7 g) were dissolved in 25 mL EG solution by magnetic stirring. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, the product was collected by an external magnetic field, washed with water and ethanol three times, and dried at 60 °C.

### 2.3. Characterization

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was taken on an FEI Inspect F50 microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 field-emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (Oxford instrument X-Max). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was collected by a Rigaku Dmax/Ultima IV diffractometer with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 1.5418 \text{ Å}$). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an XPS spectrometer (Kratos XSAM800); the binding energies were adjusted with respect to the C1s binding energy of 284.8 eV.

### 2.4. Peroxidase-like activity

The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. Typically, the reaction of OPDA (or TMB) with H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was performed at 25 °C in 100 mM phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) or 100 mM acetate buffer (pH 4.0) in the presence of 0.05 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. Unless otherwise stated, the concentrations of OPDA and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> were 0.5 and 2.0 mM respectively for the oxidation of OPDA, and the concentrations of TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> were 0.2 and 10 mM respectively for the catalytic oxidation of TMB.

### 2.5. Kinetics measurements

Kinetics measurements were performed at 25 °C with 0.05 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite in 100 mM phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) by varying the concentration of OPDA (0.2–1.5 mM) at a fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (2.0 mM) or vice versa by varying the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (0.2–6.0 mM) at a fixed concentration of OPDA (1.0 mM). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, experiments were carried out in 100 mM acetate buffer (pH 4.0) by varying the concentration of TMB (0.025–0.3 mM) at a fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (20 mM) or by varying the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (2–100 mM) at a fixed concentration of TMB (0.2 mM). The steady-state reaction rates of OPDA and TMB oxidations were obtained by monitoring the absorbance increase at 416 and 652 nm using molar absorption coefficients of 16.7 [34] and 39 mM<sup>−1</sup> cm<sup>−1</sup> [35], respectively. The apparent kinetic parameters were determined by nonlinear least square fitting of the absorbance data to Michaelis-Menten equation ($v = V_{max} \cdot [S] / (K_m + [S])$) or Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal model ($1/v = (K_m / V_{max}) \cdot (1/[S]) + 1/V_{max}$), where $v$ is the reaction rate, $V_{max}$ is the maximum rate, $[S]$ is the substrate concentration and $K_m$ is the Michaelis constant which represents the affinity of enzyme toward substrate, the value of $K_m$ is equal to the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of the maximum reaction rate.

## 3. Results and discussion

### 3.1. Characterization of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite

Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O sample. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 43.30, 50.43, 74.13° can be ascribed to the reflections of (111), (200) and (220) planes of cubic Cu (marked with ♥) with lattice constant $a = 3.615 \text{ nm}$ (JCPDS No. 65-9743). The peaks at 2θ values of 30.10, 35.46, 56.98, 62.57° can be ascribed to the reflections of (220), (311), (511), (440) planes of cubic Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> (marked with ♠) with lattice constant $a = 8.391 \text{ nm}$ (JCPDS No. 65-3107). Additionally, a weak peak at 36.50° (marked with ♦) can be indexed to the (111) plane of Cu<sub>2</sub>O (JCPDS No. 65-3288). The relatively lower intensity for the diffraction peaks of Cu<sub>2</sub>O may be due to the tiny amounts of Cu<sub>2</sub>O. Therefore, Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite sample was successfully prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method.

The SEM images show that the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite are composed of uniform spherical-like morphology (Fig. 2A and B). The mean diameter of the spherical-like particles is about 50 nm as revealed by the TEM images (Fig. 2C and D). Fig. 2E shows a typical HRTEM image of the composite and the enlarged view of area F in Fig. 2E is depicted in Fig. 2F. The fringe spacings of crystallites (I) and (III) in Fig. 2F were about 0.485 and 0.293 nm, respectively, which was in agreement with the (111) plane distance of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> (0.484 nm tabulated from XRD) and the (110) plane distance of cubic Cu<sub>2</sub>O (0.301 nm tabulated from XRD), respectively, while the zone (II) was the superposition area of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Cu<sub>2</sub>O.

Elemental analysis of the as-prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was performed with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The results show that the Cu, Fe and O elements coexist in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1A, Supporting information). The EDS elemental mapping results further confirm that the Cu, Fe elements are uniformly distributed in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1B and C, Supporting information).

The elemental composition and chemical status of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite were further analyzed by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS results indicated that the composite contained Fe, Cu, O, and C (Fig. 3A). The carbon peak was due to the adventitious hydrocarbon from the XPS instrument itself. The Fe, Cu and part of O peaks were from the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O sample. Fig. 3B shows the XPS spectrum of Fe 2p of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O sample, the peaks at binding energies (BE) of 712.1 and 724.8 eV were characteristics of Fe<sup>3+</sup>, while the peaks at BE of 709.8 and 722.8 eV were characteristics of Fe<sup>2+</sup>. The satellite signal at 717.6 eV is attributed to the combination of Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup>, for the satellite signals of Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup> are at 715.5 and 719.0 eV, respectively [36,37]. The presence of Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup> in the sample further confirms the formation of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>.

The O 1s core-level spectrum is shown in Fig. 3C. The peak at 530.1 eV is in agreement with the lattice oxygen in Cu<sub>2</sub>O [38–40] and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> [41], the peak at 531.5 eV can be assigned to the presence of hydroxyl species as a result of the surface-adsorbed water on the air-exposed surface of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite according to previous studies [38,42–44].

The XPS spectrum of Cu 2p core level region for the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite displays main peaks at 933.3 and 953.1 eV which are attributed to the binding energy of Cu 2p<sub>3/2</sub> and Cu 2p<sub>1/2</sub>, respectively (Fig. 3D). The broad peaks of Cu 2p<sub>3/2</sub> and Cu 2p<sub>1/2</sub> can be further divided into two different peaks at 932.8, 934.9 eV and 952.6, 955.1 eV, respectively. The peaks at 932.8 and 952.6 eV can be attributed to Cu/Cu<sub>2</sub>O (Cu<sup>0</sup>/Cu<sup>+</sup>) [45], whereas the peaks at 934.9 and 955.1 eV can be assigned to CuO/Cu(OH)<sub>2</sub> (Cu<sup>2+</sup>) [44,46]. Cu<sup>2+</sup> can also be distinguished by the appearance of the shake-up satellite features at ~940.6–943.5 eV [47] and ~961.9 eV (denoted by arrows), which evidenced the open 3d<sup>9</sup> shell of Cu(II) [46,48]. The binding energy at 934.9 for Cu(II) was higher than the previously reported values for CuO in the range of 933.4–933.9 eV [40,48]. It has been reported that when the content of CuO is low, the binding energies of Cu<sup>2+</sup> will shift to higher values [49]. Thus the binding energy of CuO at 934.9 eV in the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O samples may be due to the small content of Cu<sup>2+</sup> on the sample surfaces [50]. From these results it is inferred that the surface of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O sample is oxidized after being exposed to air conditions. In fact, it has been reported that tiny amounts of Cu<sub>2</sub>O on the surface could be oxidized to CuO and/or Cu(OH)<sub>2</sub> species when the surface is exposed in air with humidity [30], and the tiny surface CuO/Cu(OH)<sub>2</sub> can be detected by XPS, but not by XRD. Similar phenomenon has also been observed in our previous work that Cu<sup>2+</sup> signal can be detected by XPS on the surface of Cu<sub>2</sub>O, but CuO and Cu(OH)<sub>2</sub> are not detected by XRD [27]. Based on the differences between the XRD and XPS results, it can be reasoned that XPS is much more sensitive than XRD for surface detection. It has also been reported that XPS only detects signals within the upper 5 nm thickness on the surface, while the XRD can detect signals in micro-order thickness [30,51].

Fig. 1. XRD pattern of the as-prepared sample.

Fig. 2. The SEM (A and B), TEM (C and D) and HRTEM (E and F) images of the as-prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite.

Fig. 3. XPS spectra of the as-prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O sample: (A) survey, (B) Fe 2p, (C) O 1s and (D) Cu 2p.

### 3.2. Peroxidase activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite

The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. OPDA and TMB have been widely used as typical chromogenic substrates for investigating the oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities of natural enzymes or mimetic enzymes [6,34,52]. As shown in the insets of Fig. 4A and B, the solutions of OPDA and TMB could react with H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to present yellow and blue color, respectively, within 60 min in the presence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite, whereas, the solutions of OPDA and TMB were still colorless in the absence of the nanocomposite.

The absorption spectrum of OPDA oxidized by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in the presence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite displayed a characteristic absorption band at ~416 nm in the visible region (Fig. 4A) due to the formation of 2,3-diaminophenazine (Fig. 4C) [34], while the TMB oxidation product showed two characteristic bands at ~370 and ~652 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 4B) due to the formation of diamine and diimine as shown in Fig. 4D [52]. However, the solutions of OPDA and TMB showed no obvious absorption in their UV–Vis spectra in the absence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite (Fig. 4A and B). These results suggested that the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates OPDA and TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>.

Fig. 4. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of (A) OPDA and (B) TMB oxidation products in the presence and absence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite (Insets: The photographs of OPDA and TMB solutions oxidized by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in the presence and absence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite). The chemical processes of (C) OPDA and (D) TMB oxidation.

### 3.3. Identification of active component

Previously, it has been reported that Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles have peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates such as OPDA and TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> [14]. In order to identify the active component in the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite, we prepared Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O samples (see Supporting information and Fig. S2) and measured their peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S3, the activity of Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O, without the presence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>, was a little bit lower than that of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O. However, without the presence of Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> was dramatically decreased. In addition, the activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O was also a little bit higher than that of the sum of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O. These results suggest that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O mainly come from Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O and synergistic effect may exist between Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O composite.

It was worth of pointing out that the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles reported here was much lower than that of previous report [14]. By carefully comparing the experiment conditions, we found that the size of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles in our experiment is about 50 nm, while ~30 nm in the literature, and the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration used in our experiment was 2 mM, which was much lower than that used in the literature (530 mM). Therefore, the lower peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles reported here should be due to the larger particle size and the lower H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration used in our experiment.

Fig. 5. Comparison of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite with Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O for the oxidation of OPDA.

### 3.4. Effects of pH, catalyst concentration, temperature and time of incubation

As we know, the catalytic activity of natural enzymes, such as HRP, is dependent on solution pH, catalyst concentration and temperature, etc. So we examined these factors on the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. The effect of pH was firstly examined with the catalytic oxidation of OPDA in 100 mM phosphate buffer or acetate buffer. The solution pH was adjusted by addition of a small amount of HCl or NaOH solution. For the oxidation of OPDA, the reaction activity increased with increase in solution pH from 4.0 to 8.0, and then decreased with further increase the solution pH up to 10.0 (Fig. 6A). As of TMB oxidation, the highest reaction activity was observed at pH 4.0 (Fig. S4). This was similar to the effects of pH on the peroxidase activity of HRP and other inorganic materials [53]. The effect of catalyst concentration was studied at the fixed concentrations of OPDA (0.5 mM) and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (2 mM). When Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O was varied in the range of 0.02–0.05 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>, the reaction rate was almost proportional to the catalyst dose, but the rate was not significantly increased when further increase in catalyst concentration up to 0.075 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> (Fig. 6B).

The effect of temperature on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite was investigated by varying temperature in the range of 25–60 °C. As shown in Fig. 6C, the activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O increased with temperature below 45 °C, while decreased with further increase in temperature. It was worth pointing out that when the oxidation reaction was carried out at 60 °C, the color of the oxidation product began to fade after several minutes, indicating that the colored product was easily to be degraded by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in the presence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. This might be one of the reasons that the catalytic activity was decreased at high temperature, especially higher than 50 °C. Similar phenomena are also observed by other researchers when Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles [14] and CuS nanoparticles [54] were used as mimetic peroxidases.

The storage of natural enzymes (proteins), especially enzyme solutions, usually requires low temperature (Usually −80 to 4 °C), for natural enzymes is apt to be deteriorated at high temperature, even at room temperature conditions. To test the temperature stability of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite, we measured the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite after incubation in water at 40 °C. More than 87% catalytic activity of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite could be retained after 30 h of incubation (Fig. 6D). Furthermore, the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite could still retain more than 90% of its initial peroxidase-like activity within a storage period of three weeks in water at room temperature, further indicating the good temperature stability of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite as mimetic peroxidase.

Fig. 6. The effects of solution pH (A), catalyst dose (B), reaction of temperature (C) and time of incubation (D) on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. Experiments were carried out at standard conditions as stated in the experimental section of 2.4, except for the parameter investigated.

### 3.5. Steady-state kinetics study

Steady-state kinetics assays were carried out to further understand the mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. The rates of product formation of OPDA and TMB oxidations were determined from the increases in the absorbances at 416 [34] and 652 nm [35], respectively. The experiments were performed with fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and varying the concentration of OPDA (or TMB) or vice versa in the presence of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S5, the plots of reaction rate vs. OPDA (or TMB) and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration show typical Michaelis-Menten behavior [55]. The kinetic parameters, such as the Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and maximum velocity ($V_{max}$) were obtained by using the nonlinear least square fitting of the data to Michaelis-Menten equation and also by using the Lineweaver-Burk plots of the double reciprocal model. Data from both models were in good agreement and were summarized in Table 1 and Table S1. By comparing with the kinetic parameters of other reported inorganic materials and natural peroxidase HRP (Table 1 and Table S1), it can be seen that the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and shows good affinity toward OPDA, TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. For example, The $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ values were 0.85 mM and $13.1 \times 10^{−8} \text{ M s}^{−1}$ for OPDA and 2.3 mM and $11.9 \times 10^{−8} \text{ M s}^{−1}$ for H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, respectively. These values are comparable to those of natural peroxidase HRP (Table 1).

In addition, the change of the maximum absorbance of OPDA oxidation at 416 nm was linear increase with the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> from 0.4 to 1.5 mM with limit of detection of 0.2 mM (Fig. S6A). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, the absorbance change at 652 nm was linear increase with the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> from 4 mM to 50 mM with a limit of detection of 2 mM (Fig. S6B). These results suggest that a colorimetric method for the detection of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> could be developed based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite.

Fig. 7. Steady-state kinetics analysis of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Cu@Cu<sub>2</sub>O nanocomposite for OPDA oxidation. (A and C) the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was fixed (2.0 mM) and the concentration of OPDA was varied (0.2–1.5 mM). (B and D) the concentration of OPDA was fixed (1.0 mM) and the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was varied (0.2–6.0 mM). The curves in panels A and B were obtained by nonlinear least square fitting to Michaelis-Menten equation, $v = V_{max} \cdot [S] / (K_m + [S])$, and the lines in panels C and D were obtained by linear least square fitting to Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal medel, $1/v = (K_m / V_{max}) \cdot (1/[S]) + 1/V_{max}$.
